content:
{
  "title": "Adversarially Contrastive Prompting: Dynamic Contextual Counterprompting for Robust LLMs",
  "problem": "Current large language models (LLMs) are highly susceptible to prompt-based adversarial attacks and jailbreaks, even when defense-oriented prompts are used. Existing prompting methods often assume a static or single-view prompt construction.",
  "motivation": "As LLMs are increasingly deployed in sensitive domains, their vulnerability to adversarial inputs poses severe security and ethical risks. Prompt engineering defense strategies have plateaued, necessitating new, adaptive approaches to outpace evolving attack techniques. It's urgent to develop prompting schemes that anticipate and neutralize adversarial intent in real time.",
  "method": "We propose Adversarially Contrastive Prompting (ACP), a method where every user input is paired with a dynamically generated counterprompt that reframes the input in a slightly perturbed, adversarial direction (e.g., reworded to hint at a jailbreak or harmful intent). The LLM is prompted with both the original and the counterprompt, and required to produce outputs only if its responses to both are aligned with safety constraints. This dual-prompting scheme leverages the model's own contextual self-critique to flag and neutralize ambiguous or adversarial instructions before output generation. The counterprompt is constructed via a lightweight, on-the-fly adversarial paraphraser.",
  "experiment_plan": "We will evaluate ACP on public adversarial prompt datasets (e.g., jailbreak, toxic instruction corpora), comparing against static defense prompts and retrieval-augmented methods. Metrics will include attack success rate, response harmlessness (e.g., via OpenAI or Detoxify classifiers), and task completion. Ablation studies will assess the impact of different counterprompt generation strategies."
}
